coredata.dc:title: Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission for sensors and cells imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Diao, Haipeng
Li, Tingting
Zhang, Rong
Kang, Yu
Liu, Wen
Cui, Yanhua
Wei, Shuangyan
Wang, Ning
Li, Lihong
Wang, Haojiang
Niu, Weifen
Sun, Tijian
coredata.dc:description: Most carbon dots (CDs) conventional fabrication approaches produce single colored fluorescent materials, different methods are required to synthesize distinct carbon dots for specific optical applications. Herein, using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Syringa obtata Lindl, a facile, low-cost and green assay is achieved in the controllable synthesis of blue and green fluorescent carbon dots. The fluorescent emission of CDs can be well-tuned by adding sodium hydroxide in the precursor solution. Blue fluorescent CDs are applied to Fe3+ sensing with a low detection limit of 0.11 μM of linear range from 0.5 to 80 μM, and then further extended to analysis river water samples. Green fluorescent CDs can be applied to pH detection, which show a remarkable linear enhancement in the green fluorescence emission region when the pH is increased from 1.98 to 8.95. Eventually, the detection of Fe3+ and pH are applied for the living cells fluorescent images in MCF-7 cells are achieved successfully, indicating as-synthesized CDs potential toward diverse application as promising candidate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence carbon dots
Tunable emission
Sensors
Cells imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Recognition of CDs to Fe3+
The Response of CDs to pH
Fe3+ and pH Dependent Intracellular Fluorescence Imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for as-synthesized CDs from Syringa obtata Lindl, along with graphic of the corresponding B-CDs of under irradiating 365 nm UV lamp (top) and G-CDs of 470 nm excitation (bottom).

TEM images of B-CDs (A) and G-CDs (B), scale bar: 20 nm (Inset: HRTEM images of B-CDs and G-CDs, scale bar: 5 nm). Particle distribution of B-CDs (C) and G-CDs (D). XRD patterns of B-CDs and G-CDs (E).
FTIR spectrum of CDs (A). XPS spectrum of B-CDs (B). C1s (C), N1s (D) and O1s (E) spectra of B-CDs. XPS spectrum of G-CDs (F). C1s (G) and O1s (H) spectra of G-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of B-CDs (A) and G-CDs (B). (The insets of panel A: the images of B-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm excitation; the insets of panel B display the images of G-CDs under daylight and 470 nm excitation.). Excitation dependent emission spectra of the B-CDs (C) and G-CDs (D). (The inset displays corresponding normalized spectra.).

Effect of different metal ions on F0/F of B-CDs solution (1.2 mg/mL) at 425 nm (A). Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (B). Plot of F0/F versus Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0.5–80 μM (C). Effect of different metal ions on F0/F of G-CDs solution (1.6 mg/mL) at 520 nm (D).

Dependence of the PL intensity of B-CDs (A) and G-CDs (B) on the pH values from 1.98 to 11.92. Plot of PL intensity of G-CDs at 520 nm versus pH from 1.98 to 8.95 (C). Changes in PL intensity of G-CDs solution at 520 nm between pH 1.98 and 8.95 (D).

Cells fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells cultured in B-CDs at 37 °C for 3 h without Fe3+ (A). Dynamic fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells incubated in B-CDs and subjected to 100 μM Fe3+ for 10 s (B), 1 min (C), 5 min (D), 10 min (E). (F) Intracellular fluorescence intensities of B-CDs in designated regions shown in B–E. Fluorescence image of B-CDs treated cells after incubation with Fe3+ (100 μM) for 1 h (G), fluorescence image after further incubation with apoferritin (25 and 50 μM) for 1 h (H and I), respectively. The excitation wavelength was set to 405 nm, and fluorescence emission was collected from 425 to 475 nm.

Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells incubated with G-CDs for 1 h and kept in different B-R buffers (pH 7.40 (A), 8.95 (D) and 4.10 (G)). Bright-field image (B) and merged image (C) of A and B at pH 7.40; Bright-field image (E) and merged image (F) of D and E at pH 8.95; Bright-field image (H) and merged image (I) of G and H at pH 4.10. The excitation wavelength was set to 488 nm, and emission was collected in the green channel (495–535 nm).
Detection results of Fe3+ in river water with the proposed method and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission for sensors and cells imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Provincial People's Hospital, Taiyuan 030012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Forensic Medicine, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most carbon dots (CDs) conventional fabrication approaches produce single colored fluorescent materials, different methods are required to synthesize distinct carbon dots for specific optical applications. Herein, using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Syringa obtata Lindl, a facile, low-cost and green assay is achieved in the controllable synthesis of blue and green fluorescent carbon dots. The fluorescent emission of CDs can be well-tuned by adding sodium hydroxide in the precursor solution. Blue fluorescent CDs are applied to Fe3+ sensing with a low detection limit of 0.11 μM of linear range from 0.5 to 80 μM, and then further extended to analysis river water samples. Green fluorescent CDs can be applied to pH detection, which show a remarkable linear enhancement in the green fluorescence emission region when the pH is increased from 1.98 to 8.95. Eventually, the detection of Fe3+ and pH are applied for the living cells fluorescent images in MCF-7 cells are achieved successfully, indicating as-synthesized CDs potential toward diverse application as promising candidate.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials are important fluorescent probes, Such as quantum dots [1,2], metal nanoclusters [3] and carbon dots [4–6], which can be used to detect different types of target molecules. Due to its high aqueous solubility and photostability, excellent biocompatibility, easy preparation and functionalization, Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted wide attention originate from their applications in photocatalysis [7,8], sensors [9–11], fluorescent ink [12], drug delivery [13,14] and bioimaging [15,16].

Compared to noble metal quantum dots and nanoclusters, carbon dots benefit from their fabrication low cost and simplicity. Various methods or routs have been reported for efficient prepared of this material [17–21]. However, most of these CDs emit blue fluorescence which could interfere with the fluorescent signals from cells and tissues. In recent years, various endeavors have been made toward realizing larger-scale production, improving quantum yield, simplifying operation or lowering the cost of CDs. As far as we know, tunable emission character of CDs has been less reported. Moreover, all these methods have some drawbacks such as using expensive/toxic starting materials and solvents or tedious synthetic methodology [22–26]. So, the development of green and fast methods to synthesis nontoxic CDs with color-switchable fluorescence from natural sources are still challenging.

In recent years, the trend to synthesis CDs from natural biomass has been reported originate from nontoxic, inexhaustible and inexpensive of as-prepared CDs which have various potential applications [10,27,28]. To synthesis tunable fluorescent CDs from eco-friendly natural materials using facile ways have been deeply attracted our attention. Syringa obtata Lindl petals contain carbohydrates, protein, phenylpropanoids and flavonoid [29], which are inexhaustible, low-cost and nontoxic. In the present work, a simply, low-cost and eco-friendly strategy is performed for the first time using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Syringa obtata Lindl for synthesis of blue and green fluorescent CDs (B-CDs and G-CDs). The emission of fluorescent carbon dots can be well-tuned by adding sodium hydroxide. B-CDs are used as Fe3+ sensor and G-CDs are applied to pH detection. Eventually, the detection of Fe3+ and pH are successfully applied for the living cells fluorescent images in MCF-7 cells.

3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich corporation. AgNO3, KCl, CaCl2, NaCl, CdCl2, CuCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2, Hg(NO3)2, PbCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, CrCl3, FeCl3, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent corporation. The Syringa obtata Lindl were picked from Shanxi Medical University (Taiyuan, China) and washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. All aqueous solutions (≥19 MΩ·cm) were obtained with ultrapure water from a Smart-N system (Heal Force, China).

UV–Visible absorption spectra were performed by the UV-6100 spectrophotometer (Mapada, China). All photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded on the Varian fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, America) in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence photographs of B-CDs were performed with ZF-6 ultraviolet analyzer under 365 nm UV light (Jiapeng, Shanghai). Fluorescence photographs of G-CDs were obtained by a molecular fluorescence imaging instrument (MFI-10) under excitation of 450 nm. Fourier Transform infrared spectra were performed using Varian FTIR-640 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image was obtained using a FEI TECNAI F20 electron microscope at 200 KV. The samples for TEM image were prepared on a super-thin carbon-coated copper grid by coating the CDs dispersion, and dried for 6 h at 50 °C. The particle distribution of as-prepared CDs was obtained by managing about 100 particles. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation, were performed on an advance Bruker powder X-ray diffractometer. Meanwhile, functional groups and elemental analysis were acquired by an AXIS ULTRA DLD Shimadzu X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using 300 W Al-Kα radiations.

The B-CDs were obtained using Syringa obtata Lindl as carbon precursor via one-pot hydrothermal method. The Syringa obtata Lindl were first grounded into powder. Syringa powders (1.0 g) were added in ultrapure water (20 mL), and then the mixture was moved into a Teflon autoclave (25 mL), afterwards heated at 200 °C for 4 h in an oven. Finally, the suspension of CDs was performed for centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm millipore filter, the obtained CDs solutions were dialyzed against ultrapure water passed through a 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane for 48 h. The G-CDs were obtained using Syringa obtata Lindl as carbon precursor via a hydrothermal method by adding sodium hydroxide in the precursor solution. Typically, Syringa powder (1.0 g) and sodium hydroxide (0.5 g) were added into deionized water (20 mL) and then the mixture was moved into a Teflon autoclave, afterwards heated at 200 °C for 4 h. And the suspension of CDs was performed for centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm millipore filter, the pH of CDs solutions was neutralized, then the obtained CDs solutions were dialyzed passed through a 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane for 48 h.

Quinine sulfate is used as a standard (QY = 54%) in the measure. The QY of B-CDs was calculated by the established procedure. Absorbencies were kept under 0.1 to decrease the re-absorption effects at the 360 nm wavelength. The QY of the B-CDs was determined using the following equation:
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where Q is the QY, A refers to the absorbance, I is the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, and the solvent refractive index is showed by n. The subscript R refers to the corresponding parameter of the known fluorescent standard.
Rhodamin 6G was chosen as a standard in ethanol for determining the QY of G-CDs with a QY of 0.94 at 488 nm.

In a typical assay, the Fe3+ detection was tested in 10 mM PBS buffer solution at room temperature (pH = 7.40). 20 μL of B-CDs (1.2 mg/mL) were added into 2000 μL of PBS buffer, and followed with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. To determine the selectivity for Fe3+ sensing, same method was performed by the addition of different other metal ions instead of Fe3+. The fluorescence was performed under 340 nm excitation wavelength.

In order to evaluate the N-CDs-based sensor for Fe3+ ion detection in an artificial system, the present method was examined by the river water samples to replace ultrapure water for real water sample analysis. Water samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm, filtered through the 0.22 μm micron filter. Aliquots (1000 μL) of the CDs solution were spiked with Fe3+ standard solutions. And the spiked samples containing B-CDs were determined by the above described strategy and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICPE-9000, Shimadzu).

The detection of pH was determined at room temperature in the Britton-Robinson buffer (B-R buffers, pH 1.98, 2.87, 3.29, 4.10, 5.02, 6.09, 7.00, 7.96, 8.95, 9.91, 10.88, 11.20, 11.92). 100 μL of CDs solution (1.6 mg/mL) was transferred into 1900 μL B-R buffer at different pHs.

The biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of obtained CDs was explored using MTT assay. Firstly, human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were seeded in a 96-well microtiter plate and cultured in CO2 incubator for 12 h. The wells of cells were taken as a blank group without treatment with CDs. Secondly, different concentrations CDs were added into the 96-well microtiter plate, and then cultured for 24 h. Thirdly, every well was added 20 μL MTT (5.0 mg/mL), and the MCF-7 cells were cultured for 4 h. The MTT reagent was removed, and then the cells of wells were washed using PBS buffer for three times and added 150 μL of DMSO into every well. The absorbance was obtained with micro-plate reader (ELx808, Biotek) at 490 nm.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in DMEM medium at 37 °C, and then subculture using 0.05% trypsin. MCF-7 cells were subcultured onto the glass bottom Petri dish, and allowed to grow for attachment within 24 h.

The DMEM mediums contain 2.0 mg/mL B-CDs were used for culturing the MCF-7 cells. The DMEM mediums were removed after incubation for 3 h. The MCF-7 cells were washed using PBS buffer three times, and then kept in PBS for fluorescence imaging using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Olympus, Japan). Besides, B-CDs-stained MCF-7 cells were treated with Fe3+ (100 μM) for 10 min using CLSM for dynamic fluorescence imaging. The cells loaded with B-CDs/Fe3+ were further treated with apoferritin (25 and 50 μM) for 1 h for fluorescence imaging. B-CDs were excited at 405 nm and its blue fluorescence emissions were collected by a band-pass filter (425–475 nm).

The MCF-7 cells incubated with G-CDs in CO2 incubator for 1 h. After that, to remove excess G-CDs, the MCF-7 cells were washed three times using PBS buffer, and then kept in different B-R buffers (pH 4.10, 7.00 or 8.95) for fluorescence imaging using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Olympus, Japan). Green fluorescence emissions of G-CDs were collected by a 495–535 nm band-pass filter excited at 488 nm.

In Fig. 1 , using Syringa obtata Lindl as the starting materials, B-CDs and G-CDs are synthesized successfully via one-step hydrothermal method at 200 °C, 4 h, respectively. The emission of fluorescent carbon dots can be well-tuned by adding sodium hydroxide in the precursor solution. Hydrothermal synthesis under mild temperatures (150–250 °C) and self-generated pressure in a closed system avoids traditional harsh conditions. Syringa obtata Lindl petals contain carbohydrates, protein, phenylpropanoids and flavonoid [29], which are inexhaustible, low-cost and nontoxic. To get good fluorescence properties, optimization of reaction conditions (reaction time and temperature) of PL properties are investigated for the as-prepared CDs. In Fig. S1, the optimization reaction conditions are 200 °C for 4 h for B-CDs and G-CDs.

In Fig. 2A and B, TEM images show that as-prepared B-CDs and G-CDs are well dispersed and spherical in morphology. In view of the statistical analysis of CDs from TEM (about 100 particles), the size distribution of B-CDs (range from 1.0 nm to 5.0 nm) is relatively small (Fig. 2C), and the size distribution of G-CDs is bigger within the range from 2.0 to 8.0 nm (Fig. 2D), the average diameter of B-CDs is around 2.76 nm and that of G-CDs is around 4.46 nm. In addition, the HR-TEM of G-CDs show a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of ca. 0.321 nm (the inset in Fig. 2B) attributable to the graphitic nature and B-CDs have no crystalline structure (the inset in Fig. 2A) observed using HR-TEM. In Fig. 2E, a typical XRD pattern of B-CDs displays a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at around 27.2° agrees with the HR-TEM image, which is ascribed to highly disorder of carbon atoms [9]. The XRD pattern of as-synthesized G-CDs with a diffraction peak at around 23°, which is ascribed to the diffraction of the (002) lattice plane agrees with the HR-TEM image [30].

The functional groups of the as-prepared CDs are identified by the FTIR spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 3A. In FTIR spectrum of B-CDs (red line), the peaks at 3415 and 3205 cm−1 could be identified as the OH and NH stretching vibrations, the small band at 2931 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The peaks at 1660 cm−1, 1562 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and the CH2 bending vibrations, respectively. And the broad band around 1095 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of CO. Compared with B-CDs, FTIR spectrum of G-CDs (black line), but no peak appeared at 3200 cm−1 revealing the absence of NH.

The surface element analyses of CDs are also identified by the XPS [10,20]. In Fig. 3B, three typical strong peaks of the B-CDs XPS spectrum (285.4, 399.8 and 531.8 eV) belong to C1s, N1s and O1s. And the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3C) can be fitted four main peaks, and the C1s peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to CC, the peaks of C1s at about 285.7 eV, 286.5 eV and 287.9 eV can be assigned to CN, COH and CO, respectively. While the N1s expanded spectrum (Fig. 3D) can be fitted two peaks, and the peaks of N1s at 399.6 eV and 400.5 eV is ascribed to the CNC and NH. And in Fig. 3E, two peaks of the expanded XPS spectrum of O1s at 531.6 and 532.3 eV can be ascribed to the CO and COC/COH, respectively. The above XPS results verify that B-CDs are functionalized with carboxylic and amino moieties. Nevertheless, in Fig. 3F, two apparent peaks of G-CDs centered at 285.5 and 531.8 eV can be ascribed to C1s and O1s, respectively. In Fig. 3G, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be divided into three peaks at 284.6, 285.1 and 288.1 eV, which can be ascribed to CC bond, COH and CO, respectively. In Fig. 3H, three peaks of the high resolution O1s spectrum at 531.2, 532.3 and 535.5 eV can be assigned to CO, COC/COH and OCO groups, respectively. So, the functional groups of as-prepared CDs characterised by XPS are consistent with FTIR.

In Fig. 4 , the optical properties of as-synthesized CDs are studied. B-CDs and G-CDs display broad UV–Vis absorption from 200 to 500 nm, and the strong absorption peaks are (around 281 nm) corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO bond [31]. In Fig. 4A, under excitation at 340 nm, a strong fluorescence spectrum (maximum emission wavelength at 425 nm) is displayed. From the inset in Fig. 4A, under the radiation of 365 nm UV light, very bright blue fluorescence of B-CDs is observed. Quinine sulfate is used as a standard (QY = 54%), the QY of the B-CDs is calculated to be about 12.4%, demonstrating the good fluorescence characters [32]. While G-CDs show an obvious fluorescence emission at 520 nm when excited at 450 nm in Fig. 4B. In the inset of Fig. 4B, green luminescence is observed under 470 nm light. Rhodamin 6G is chosen as a standard for determining the QY of green luminescence, and the QY of G-CDs is calculated to be 6.5%.

To further study the optical properties of as-prepared CDs, under different excitation wavelengths, the PL spectra of B-CDs and G-CDs are determined (Fig. 4C and D). The as-prepared CDs display excitation-dependent fluorescence emission character. As depicted in Fig. 4C and D, with the increased excitation wavelength, the fluorescent peak red-shifted and the strongest emission spectrum of B-CDs and G-CDs are observed at the excitation wavelengths of 340 nm and 450 nm. The corresponding normalized spectras are displayed in the insets of Fig. 4C and D. These behaviors of CDs result from different surface states, which origin from the different functional groups on CDs' surface and the different sizes of nano-CDs [33].

The stability of the as-synthesized fluorescent CDs is investigated. In Fig. S2A and B, the effect of ionic strength on the PL stability of B-CDs and G-CDs is studied. The spectral feature and PL intensity of CDs change little under various concentrations of KCl. Meanwhile, in Fig. S2C–F, after irradiation for 2 h under a Xe lamp (500 W) and storage for 90 days, their PL intensity doesn't change certifying as-prepared CDs have excellent stability and excellent candidate for commercial purpose and meaningful applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Recognition of CDs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, to evaluate the sensitively and selectivity of obtained CDs in the sensing system, the PL intensity of as-prepared CDs with different metal cations are studied under physiological conditions. Various transition metal cations including Ag+, Na+, K+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe3+ and Al3+ of each (400 μM) are reacted with the as-prepared CDs. As depicted in Fig. 5A, relative to the free B-CDs, a much lower PL intensity is seen for B-CDs with addition of Fe3+ ions, either slight change or no in the PL intensity are displayed for other metal ions. Fe3+ ions show the strongest fluorescence quenching effect on B-CDs among these metal ions, which indicates that the as-prepared B-CDs could be developed as an excellent fluorescence Fe3+ sensor. Such a specific PL quenching effect may be ascribed to the strong interactions between the function groups on the as-prepared B-CDs surface and Fe3+ which lead to the quenching of the B-CDs fluorescence through photoelectrons transfer [12,34]. The Fe3+ sensing capacity (in the range of 0 to 400 μM) of B-CDs is studied under physiological condition at 340 nm excitation. Fig. 5B shows the PL quenching of B-CDs (1.2 mg/mL) at different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. The PL intensity of B-CDs decreased progressively with increasing Fe3+ ions concentration, indicating that Fe3+ concentration is sensitive to the sensing system. The inset of Fig. 5B shows the naked visible fluorescence change of the B-CDs solution by Fe3+ ions under 365 nm UV light. It is very apparent that the blue emission disappeared after adding Fe3+ (400 μM) to the B-CDs solution. As is well known, Fe3+ ions are the highest trace elements in the human body and indispensable for biochemical processes. So, to detection of Fe3+ ions is of great benefit through a naked visible method. In Fig. 5C, the quenching efficiency of B-CDs is fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q], where F and F0 are the PL intensities of B-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+, [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+ ions and Ksv is the S-V quenching constant. And it can be fitted the linear S-V equation over the concentration range of 0.5–80 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9984, while the plot cannot be fitted a linear equation over the whole range of the concentration of Fe3+ manifesting that both dynamic and static quenching processes exist in the CDs sensor system. Based on the results, the limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed B-CDs sensor is determined as 0.11 μM.

To further evaluate its practicality, the developed B-CDs-based Fe3+ sensing system are extended to determine Fe3+ in real water samples from river water. The recoveries are 95.50–105.50% (using the proposed methodology) and 98.00–101.00% (inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer), respectively (in Table 1 ). These results indicate that it is capable of detecting Fe3+ in river water with this method. Thus, the facile, low-cost and green preparative strategy provides the application for Fe3+ detection in real sample.

As shown in Fig. 5D, relative to the free G-CDs, the G-CDs PL intensities have no change even the metal ions concentration reach up to 400 μM except that Pb2+ and Hg2+ decreases slightly. These results manifest that the as-synthesized G-CDs are high tolerance to metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Response of CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: As pH plays a critical role in many biological and chemical systems, pH detection is of pivotal importance. The response of as-synthesized CDs to a wide range pH values is studied. In Fig. 6A, The PL intensity of B-CDs has no change with pH values increasing from 1.98 to 7.40. But, the PL intensity decreased dramatically when pH is higher than 7.40 with the increase of pH values. As depicted in Fig. 6B, the PL intensities of G-CDs at 520 nm enhance significantly with a broad pH range of 1.98–8.95 compared with B-CDs, suggesting that G-CDs have strong pH-dependent property. The pH-dependent property of G-CDs may ascribe to the protonation and deprotonation [35,36], the carboxylic acid moieties are deprotonated to carboxylates on the surface of G-CDs when pH increases from 1.98 to 8.95, and then a negative charged shell is formed which generates the non-radiative recombination, consequently enhancing the PL intensity.

Apparently, based on the linear equation: I
PL = 79.4819pH-30.2721, a good linearity between pH and PL intensity of G-CDs is showed at the range of 1.98–8.95 in Fig. 6C (R2 = 0.9951). The results declare that G-CDs could be used and applied to detect pH as fluorescent pH sensor within the range of 1.98–8.95. In addition, to monitor pH changes, the reversibility is highly desirable for fluorescent pH probe. In Fig. 6D, the PL intensity of G-CDs at 520 nm is determined when the pH switched between 1.98 and 8.95 back and forth for six times. The results clearly reveal that the PL intensities of G-CDs are fully reversible between pH 1.98 and 8.95. The above results clearly demonstrate this G-CDs-based pH-sensitive sensor possess the excellent reversibility.

For effective bioimaging, the selected fluorescent marker is required for optical merits and low cytotoxicity. Herein, in order to study the potential application of as-synthesized CDs in living cells, the cells cytotoxicity of CDs are tested in MCF-7 cells by the MTT assay, which is performed for B-CDs and G-CDs with various concentrations in MCF-7 cells for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S3, even with a high concentration (5.0 mg/mL) of as-synthesized CDs, the cells survival rates of B-CDs and G-CDs are higher than 85% and 80%, respectively. These results suggest that as-prepared CDs show low toxicity or nontoxic to the cells, which are potential candidates for biomedical applications and bioimaging of live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ and pH Dependent Intracellular Fluorescence Imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 7 , MCF-7 cells are incubated with B-CDs and then with Fe3+ (100 μM) for 10 min. As a control, the MCF-7 cells show strong blue fluorescence for incubating with B-CDs at 10 min without Fe3+ (Fig. 7A). Fig. 7B–E display the dynamic fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells incubated with B-CDs and Fe3+ at different time. After Fe3+ ions are added, the blue emission decreases gradually with the extended time for incubating with Fe3+ in the MCF-7 cells. Fig. 7F exhibits the intracellular fluorescence intensities of B-CDs at designated area. The results show that B-CDs could be a favorable probe for detecting Fe3+ in biosystem. The ferritin is an iron storage protein, which composed of 24 subunits and can form a hollow shell capable of encapsulating Fe atoms. Therefore, the B-CDs/Fe3+ system can be destroyed by apoferritin. As shown in Fig. 7G–I, as chelating reagent for Fe3+, apoferritin is used for cell imaging. Fig. 7G shows that B-CDs incubated with Fe3+ (100 μM) display weak intracellular fluorescence. However, as shown in Fig. 7H and I, the cells with loaded B-CDs/Fe3+ are treated with apoferritin, the brightness of cells gradually enhance with the increase of apoferritin. The above results indicate that endocytosed apoferritin has strong binding preference toward Fe3+ and compete with B-CDs to form ferritin complex, which disturbs the interaction between Fe3+ and B-CDs and lead to an “off-on” fluorescence switching.

In order to explore the pH-dependent intracellular fluorescence imaging of G-CDs, MCF-7 cells are also used as a model. Due to strong acid may influence cells viability, MCF-7 cells are incubated with G-CDs and then kept in different B-R buffers (pH 4.10, 7.00 or 8.95) for fluorescence imaging. As depicted in Fig. 8 , G-CDs emit green fluorescence within living cells, manifesting favorable cell membrane permeability. The strongest green emissions are displayed for the G-CDs stained cells at pH 7.40 (Fig. 8A and C). When the pH changed to 8.95, G-CDs-stained MCF-7 cells exhibited stronger green fluorescence (Fig. 8D and F). While the pH drops to 4.10, it is found the weaker green emissions (Fig. 8G and I). The MCF-7 cells incubated with G-CDs for the bright-field images (Fig. 8B, E and H) make clear the normal cells morphology, certificating that as-prepared CDs are possess minimum toxicity and biocompatible to the cells. The results imply that G-CDs could be potentially valuable for pH imaging in living cells.

Overall, we have first developed a facile, green, and low-cost way for fabricating fluorescence CDs with tunable emission, using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of Syringa obtata Lindl. Interestingly, two as-prepared CDs can be applied to Fe3+ sensing and H+ detection, respectively. More importantly, the excellent responses of obtained CDs to Fe3+ and pH are applied for sensing Fe3+ and H+ in vivo. This new strategy may give a new way for developing sensitive and environmentally friendly sensors in analytical and biological fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705104, 21705105), the Key Research and Development Projects of Shanxi Province (201703D321015-2), the Youth Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201701D221064), the Science & Technology Innovation Fund of Shanxi Medical University (C01201003) and the Youth Foundation of Shanxi Medical University (02201306).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.04.029.
